title: Tropical Quiver Representations
speaker: Victoria Schleis
abstract:
Grassmannians and flag varieties are important moduli spaces in algebraic geometry. Quiver Grassmannians are generalizations of these spaces arise in representation theory as the moduli spaces of quiver subrepresentations. These represent arrangements of vector subspaces satisfying linear relations provided by a directed graph.
Using tropical geometry, we introduce matroidal and tropical analoga of quivers and their Grassmannians obtained in joint work with Alessio Borzì and separate joint work with Giulia Iezzi; and describe them as affine morphisms of valuated matroids and linear maps of tropical linear spaces. Further, I will discuss first steps towards a polyhedral characterization of morphisms, and give an overview of recent work in progress on applying and extending the theory.

proof_stages.label: Classical quiver Grassmannians and incidence moduli
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines quivers, their representations and subrepresentations, and the quiver Grassmannian as a subvariety of a product of Grassmannians parametrizing linear incidence data.

proof_stages.label: Naive tropicalization versus structural tropical replacement
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates the tropical goal by replacing vector spaces with ground sets and linear subspaces with tropical linear spaces, while explaining that direct tropicalization only captures realizable tropicalizations and is computationally difficult.

proof_stages.label: Ground-set maps and valuated-matroid morphisms
proof_stages.summary: A first tropical analogue of quiver arrows is developed using maps of ground sets, induced preimage valuated matroids, a quotient condition for morphisms, and translation weights that account for diagonal rescaling.

proof_stages.label: Matrix presentations and tropical quiver Plücker equations
proof_stages.summary: An alternative approach fixes coordinates and matrices on arrows, tropicalizes the quiver Plücker-type relations, defines a tropical quiver prevariety, and interprets it as parametrizing tropical linear spaces satisfying containment after tropical matrix multiplication.

proof_stages.label: Set-map model as the nice matrix subcase
proof_stages.summary: The two tropical analogues are compared by showing that weighted ground-set maps are represented by special matrices, and in this nice subcase matrix containment is equivalent to the valuated-matroid morphism condition.

proof_stages.label: Representability failures and small counterconstructions
proof_stages.summary: Representability is then analyzed, with the conclusion that the tropical and realizable pictures diverge very quickly: nonrealizable behavior appears for ground-set size two over nontrivially valued fields and for size three in the general or trivially valued setting.

proof_stages.label: Coordinate dependence and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The talk ends by asking whether different coordinate or matrix presentations of the same classical quiver representation yield the same or related tropical quiver Grassmannians, and what information the tropical prevariety retains about the tropicalization.

examples.name: A3 quiver as a flag variety
examples.note: The linearly oriented A3 quiver with identity maps and dimension vector (1,2,3) makes the quiver Grassmannian visibly into a flag variety, illustrating the classical moduli interpretation.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker assigns vector spaces and identity maps to the A3 chain and imposes dimension vector 1,2,3 to derive the containment chain giving the flag variety

passes.reason: The identity A3 chain gives a homogeneous flag variety and is used only illustratively.

examples.name: Projection, permutation, and contraction maps of ground sets
examples.note: These elementary operations illustrate what the proposed ground-set maps can encode on valuated matroids and tropical linear spaces.
examples.engagement_reason: projection, permutation, and contraction maps are listed as kinds of ground-set maps the definition can describe, with no concrete construction run on any one of them

passes.reason: Projections and contractions forget or identify ground elements, while permutations are ground-set symmetries.
projections and contractions forget or identify ground-set directions, while permutations are coordinate symmetries
The listed ground-set maps include coordinate permutations as symmetries and projections/contractions that discard or identify directions.
Permutations are symmetry maps, while projections and contractions drop or identify ground-set elements.

The list includes coordinate-forgetting or identifying maps, while permutations are coordinate symmetries.
Used as available map types, with permutations giving symmetries and projections/contractions forgetting or identifying coordinates.

Projection and contraction ground-set maps collapse or forget coordinates, while permutations are coordinate symmetries, and the maps are illustrative rather than adversarial.
These are positive examples of allowed ground-set maps, with permutations giving coordinate symmetries and projections/contractions forgetting or identifying directions.
These maps are explanatory examples, with permutations giving symmetries and projections/contractions collapsing ground-set data.
These standard ground-set maps illustrate the formalism, with projections/contractions collapsing coordinates and permutations giving coordinate symmetries.

examples.name: Matrix image of a tropical line missing a ray
examples.note: Multiplying a classical tropical line by the displayed matrix produces a cut-off tropical line, showing why arbitrary tropical matrix multiplication does not preserve tropical linear spaces and motivating caution in the matrix approach.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies a specific matrix to the classical tropical line and states the resulting cut-off tropical line missing the y-ray

examples.name: Known nonrealizable Grassmannian and flag-variety cases
examples.note: Previously known nonrealizable examples for tropical Grassmannians and flag varieties provide scale and contrast for the stronger small-size failures in the quiver setting.
